title: Asynchronous Architecture Decisions for Go Services · Go Semantics Case: Temporal Coupling Decision
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must make coupling, buffering, fan-out, replay, and failure costs observable at Go service boundaries; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the temporal coupling decision increment in a Go service coupling and broker-fit decision record. Learners progress through…
objectives: Choose asynchronous messaging only when temporal decoupling, buffering, fan-out, failure isolation, or replay creates measurable value; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Plan browser models, Go compilation, vet, table tests, fuzzing, race and leak checks, disposable RabbitMQ integration, partition, restart, load, upgrade, and production transfer gates.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Temporal Coupling Decision · Theory Temporal Coupling Decision
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 7507 during outbox relay recovery after a database commit. Build deterministic Go evidence to choose HTTP, work distribution, pub/sub, or retained streams from coupling evidence; name the owner and stop path for every goroutine and notification channel; then lose a quorum member and recompute majority availability. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Temporal Coupling Decision contract for the supplied data.
